moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, establishing central presence and preparing the classic d4+c4 pawn duo.
plans: Follow up with c4 for a broad center
Develop pieces systematically
alternatives: e4 — more open, tactical games

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to its most natural square, controlling e4 and maintaining maximum flexibility. From here Black can go into dozens of different defenses depending on how White continues.
plans: Control e4 and react to White's setup
Keep all defensive options open
alternatives: d5 — immediately contests the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: White grabs queenside space and controls d5 with the c-pawn. The d4+c4 duo is the most ambitious setup against Indian defenses.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The d4+c4 formation gives White a space advantage and central control.
plans: Support the center with Nc3
Push e4 if Black allows it
alternatives: Nf3 — more flexible, delays c4

moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the signature move of the Old Indian Defence. Instead of the modern g6 (King's Indian) or e6 (Nimzo/Queen's Indian), Black plays d6 — a solid, old-school approach that keeps the center close.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The d6 pawn supports a future e5 advance and keeps the center compact.
plans: Prepare the e5 break to challenge White's center
Develop solidly before committing to a plan
alternatives: g6 — King's Indian, more popular and aggressive

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to its ideal square, reinforcing control of d5 and e4. The knight on c3 is the backbone of White's central strategy — it supports the e4 push and keeps a grip on d5.
plans: Support e4 to build a massive center
Maintain d5 control
alternatives: Nf3 — also natural, but Nc3 puts more immediate pressure on the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7 rather than c6. Why d7 instead of c6? Because in the Old Indian, Black wants to play e5, and a knight on c6 would block the c-pawn from supporting the center.
plans: Support the e5 advance
Reroute the knight to c5 or f8 depending on the position
alternatives: c6 — possible but blocks the c-pawn and commits to a different structure

moveAnnotations.annotation: White seizes the full center with e4, achieving the ideal d4+e4+c4 pawn trio. This is exactly what White wants in the Old Indian — a massive spatial advantage.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's d4+e4+c4 is the classical broad center, dominating the board.
plans: Use the space advantage to develop an attack
Prepare f3 and Be3 for a complete setup
alternatives: Nf3 — more modest, but White should seize the center when given the chance

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes back at the center immediately — this is the whole point of the Old Indian. The e5 push challenges White's d4 pawn directly and fights for breathing room.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After e5, the center tension determines the entire middlegame character.
plans: Challenge White's broad center
If the center locks, launch a kingside attack
alternatives: g6 — transposes to a King's Indian setup, but e5 is the Old Indian's identity

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight and supports d4 before the tension in the center is resolved. The knight on f3 is the natural defender of d4 and also controls the e5 square.
plans: Maintain central tension
Complete development with Be2 and O-O
alternatives: d5 — closes the center immediately, leading to a King's Indian-style pawn race

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a solid and flexible square. In the Old Indian, the bishop on e7 isn't as aggressive as g7 in the King's Indian, but it serves important defensive duties — it covers.
plans: Castle kingside quickly
Reposition the bishop based on how the center resolves
alternatives: g6 — fianchetto, but that's really the King's Indian at this point

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to e2, a safe and flexible square that prepares castling. The bishop on e2 doesn't commit to an aggressive diagonal yet — it can later go to d3 or f3 if needed.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Later decide between f3+g4 kingside plans or queenside expansion
alternatives: Bd3 — more aggressive, but can be hit by Nc5 later

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles to safety. With the king secured, Black can now focus on the middlegame plan.
plans: Prepare a central or kingside break
Activate the rooks

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles, completing the basic development. Both sides are now fully castled with pieces on natural squares. The real strategic battle begins now.
plans: Choose between queenside expansion (b4) or kingside attack (f4)
Use the space advantage to create threats

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prepares the key d5 break by supporting it with the c-pawn. This is the most important strategic idea in the Old Indian — Black needs to challenge the center with d5 at some point, and c6 is t.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: c6 prepares d5, which would liquidate the center and free Black's pieces.
plans: Play d5 at the right moment to break open the center
Redirect the knight via b6 to c5 or a4
alternatives: Re8 — preparing the break more slowly

moveAnnotations.annotation: White closes the center with d5, a critical strategic decision. By pushing d5, White locks the center and the game becomes a race: White attacks on the queenside with a4-a5-b4, while Black attacks o.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The closed d5-e4 vs e5-d6 structure creates a clear strategic plan for both sides: White pushes qu.
plans: Launch queenside operations with a4, b4, c5
Use the space advantage to break through on the a and b files
alternatives: dxe5 — opens the position but gives Black active pieces

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black locks down the queenside with c5, preventing White's b4 expansion and creating a permanent pawn wedge.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The c5+d6+e5 chain is rock solid, blocking White's queenside ambitions and directing all the actio.
plans: Launch a kingside attack with f5, g5, Nf8-g6
Use the d4 square as an outpost for a knight
alternatives: Nc5 — active piece play, but c5 is the more principled structural move
